question: Does everyone who receives the mark of the beast end up lost? What about those who were deceived (Rev 19:20)? Does God guarantee fair warning before the mark crisis? Can the mark be resisted (Rev 15:2)? How does 2 Thessalonians 2:10-12 explain the relationship between deception and accountability — is the "strong delusion" a cause of truth-rejection or a consequence? Trace the judicial delusion pattern: Romans 1:24-28, Psalm 81:11-12, Isaiah 6:9-10. How does graduated accountability work (Luke 12:47-48, Acts 17:30, 1 Tim 1:13)? How does the OT ignorance-vs-presumption distinction (Lev 4, Num 15:22-31) establish the framework? Is the close of probation taught in the Bible — how do Rev 22:11, Rev 15:8, and Daniel 12:1 converge?
prior_research_summary: Six prior studies are directly relevant. mark-beast-deception-warning covers the exact same question comprehensively — judicial delusion pattern, graduated accountability, mark finality, fair warning — but did not engage the 3AM series context (studies 14-16) or the evangelical critique of close of probation. knowledge-accountability-judgment established a four-level hierarchy of accountability. man-of-sin-2thess-2 provided detailed Greek analysis of 2 Thess 2:9-12. 3am-14 through 3am-16 provide the worship-binary framework, charagma/sphragis identification, and wrath-without-mixture theology that this study must complete by addressing the JUSTICE of the third angel's warning. Web research confirmed scholarly consensus that the strong delusion is consequence not cause, and surfaced Tinker's evangelical critique that close of probation is extra-biblical. This study must go DEEPER with fresh tool-driven research on the close-of-probation biblical case and engage Tinker's critique.

required_verses.reason: Central judicial-delusion passage — causal chain from truth-rejection to divine delusion
Three-fold paradidomi — progressive judicial abandonment pattern
OT prototype of judicial abandonment — 'I gave them over to their stubborn heart'
Hiphil imperatives — judicial hardening of Israel's heart/ears/eyes
Proof the mark CAN be resisted — nikountas ek tou theriou
Differentiated judgment — deceivers vs deceived receive different fates
Close-of-probation text — 'let him be unjust still' — imperative, permissive, or declarative?
Temple filled with smoke, no one could enter — cessation of intercession?
Michael stands up, everyone found written in the book — pre-advent finalization?
Many vs few stripes — graduated punishment based on knowledge
God 'overlooked' (hupereidon) times of ignorance — now commands repentance
Paul obtained mercy because he acted in ignorance and unbelief
OT framework — inadvertent sin (bishgagah) vs high-hand sin (beyad ramah)
Graduated sin offerings — priest/ruler/commoner with different sacrifices
First angel's message — universal gospel guarantee before judgment
Third angel's warning — the specific penalty for mark reception
'If I had not come and spoken, they had not had sin' — knowledge creates culpability
'If ye were blind, ye should have no sin' — genuine ignorance removes guilt
'Willful sin after epignosis of truth' — no remaining sacrifice; connects Num 15 to NT
'Repented not' refrain — habitual refusal pattern
Second 'repented not' refrain during plagues — defiance confirmed
Judged by the standard received — Gentiles by conscience, Jews by law
'Come out of her my people' — God calls His own out BEFORE judgment
Gospel preached in all the world before the end — universal witness guarantee
Wisdom called, they refused — then calamity comes and no answer
'My people are destroyed for lack of knowledge' — rejected knowledge leads to rejection
'Ephraim is joined to idols: let him alone' — divine withdrawal from persistent idolater
'Better not to have known the way of righteousness' — knowledge amplifies judgment
'To him that knoweth to do good and doeth it not, to him it is sin'
Coercion mechanism — death penalty + economic exclusion; hina + subjunctive = attempted compliance
Overcoming by blood of the Lamb and word of testimony — means of resistance
Spirit striving has limits — 'My spirit shall not always strive with man'
Sabbath as sign between God and Israel — seal connection
Sabbath as sign of sanctification — seal background
Tav on foreheads of those who sigh over abominations — sealing precedent
'More tolerable for Sodom' — proportional judgment based on light received
'Father forgive them, they know not what they do' — ignorance invoked at crucifixion
'Through ignorance ye did it' — Peter invokes ignorance for crucifiers
Paresis — God 'passed over' former sins in forbearance
'Other sheep I have' — God guarantees reaching His own in every fold
'Ye shall seek me and find me when ye search with all your heart'
'Ask and it shall be given' — seeking guarantees finding
'If any man will do his will, he shall know of the doctrine'
'The grace of God that bringeth salvation hath appeared to all men'
'The true light which lighteth every man' — universal light channel
Creation witness — 'without excuse' from natural revelation
Goodness of God leadeth to repentance — divine patience
'Not willing that any should perish' — divine reluctance to condemn
Three negations define the saved — no worship, no image, no mark; binary outcome
Unpardonable sin — blasphemy against the Holy Spirit
Impossible to renew to repentance after full enlightenment rejected
'Sin unto death' — some sin is beyond intercession
Reversibility hint — eyes opened, ears unstopped (counterbalances Isa 6:9-10)
Shut door — 'I know you not' after probationary period
'Strive to enter' — door shuts, no reentry
Angel casts censer — possible close-of-probation marker
NT quotation of Isa 6:9-10 in parable context
Shorter form of Isa 6:9-10 — parables as judicial function
Additional categories of inadvertent sin requiring trespass offering
Eli's house — iniquity not purged by sacrifice forever (unpardonable category)
Spirit convicts the world of sin, righteousness, judgment — universal conviction
'Blessed is he that watcheth' — warning placed DURING sixth plague; implies probation
Vessels of wrath fitted to destruction — divine patience endured
Contrast — 'God hath chosen you to salvation' vs those who perish in vv.10-12
Instance of probation closing — Israel's opportunity expired, attempted reversal failed
Esau found no place of repentance though he sought it with tears
God sent messengers 'until there was no remedy' — exhausted patience
'This iniquity shall not be purged from you till ye die' — irrevocable judgment
'Pray not for this people' — intercession limit reached
'Though Moses and Samuel stood before me' — past the point of intercession

focus_areas.title: The causal chain in 2 Thessalonians 2:10-12 — delusion as consequence
focus_areas.what: Determine whether the 'strong delusion' is a cause of truth-rejection or a judicial consequence of prior truth-rejection
focus_areas.why: This is the pivot passage for the entire study — if delusion is caused by God arbitrarily, the deceived are victims; if it is a response to prior choice, they are accountable
focus_areas.how: Parse vv.10-12 Greek, trace anth' hon + dia touto causal markers, examine energeian planes
focus_areas.narrative: The grammar reference passage entry at $GRAMMAR_REF/passages/2thess-2-1-12.md already provides verse-by-verse parsing tables — READ it first. The critical causal chain is: v.10 anth' hon ('because') + ouk edexanto (aorist middle indicative of dechomai — ACTIVE refusal, not passive failure) + v.11 dia touto ('for this cause') + pempei (present active indicative — God actively sends). Run greek_parser.py on 2 Thess 2:10-12. READ $WORD_STUDIES/G1753-energeia.md (energeia — supernatural working) and $WORD_STUDIES/G4106-plane.md (plane — wandering/error/delusion). The compound energeian planes is a genitive of quality: 'a working characterized by delusion.' Compare kat' energeian tou Satana in v.9 — Satan's energeia produces deception; God's energeia produces delusion as judicial response. Check whether edexanto (v.10) is truly aorist middle (active refusal) or could be parsed differently. The research agent should also retrieve $WORD_STUDIES/G225-aletheia.md for the ten agapen tes aletheias construction — what is the 'love of the truth' that they did not receive?

focus_areas.title: The judicial abandonment pattern — Romans 1:24-28, Psalm 81:11-12, Isaiah 6:9-10
focus_areas.what: Trace the consistent biblical pattern where God 'gives over' or 'hardens' people AFTER they have persistently rejected truth
focus_areas.why: This pattern is the OT-NT theological backbone proving that divine hardening is judicial, not arbitrary
focus_areas.how: Parse Rom 1:24,26,28 paradidomi, Psa 81:11-12 Hebrew, Isa 6:9-10 hiphil imperatives
focus_areas.narrative: Three passages form a unified judicial-abandonment theology. In Romans 1:24,26,28, paradidomi (G3860) appears three times — READ $WORD_STUDIES/G3860-paradidomi.md. Andrews (2025) argues this carries 'legal and punitive weight' — verify by running greek_parser.py on Rom 1:24, 1:26, 1:28 to confirm all three are the same verb form. Note the escalation: lusts of hearts (v.24) → dishonorable passions (v.26) → debased mind (v.28, adokimon noun from dokimazo — 'they did not approve/test [edokimasan] God in knowledge, so He gave them over to an unapproved [adokimon] mind'). For Psalm 81:11-12, run hebrew_parser.py on both verses — check the Hebrew verb behind 'gave them over' (shalach = send away/release) and compare conceptually with paradidomi. For Isaiah 6:9-10, READ $GRAMMAR_REF/hebrew/stem-hiphil.md for the causative stem, then run hebrew_parser.py on Isa 6:10 to confirm hashmen (hiphil of shaman), hakbed (hiphil of kabed), hasha (hiphil of sha'a). The key interpretive question: are these hiphils causative ('God directly hardens') or resultative/permissive ('God confirms their chosen state')? The NT quotations vary — compare Matt 13:14-15 (LXX tradition) with Mark 4:10-12 (shorter form). Run cross_testament_parallels_v2.py --hybrid-nt on Isa 6:9 to find all NT quotations.

focus_areas.title: The OT ignorance-vs-presumption distinction — Leviticus 4 and Numbers 15:22-31
focus_areas.what: Establish the OT framework distinguishing inadvertent sin (atonement available) from high-hand sin (no atonement, cut off)
focus_areas.why: This is the foundational framework for all NT graduated-accountability texts and the mark-of-the-beast culpability question
focus_areas.how: Parse Num 15:22-31 Hebrew vocabulary, Lev 4 categories, connect to Heb 10:26-29
focus_areas.narrative: The pivotal Hebrew terms are bishgagah (H7684, from H7683 shagag — 'to stray/err') and beyad ramah ('with a high/raised hand' — H3027 yad + H7311 ruwm). READ $WORD_STUDIES/H7683-shagag.md and $WORD_STUDIES/H7686-shagah.md for the root semantics. READ $WORD_STUDIES/H3027-yad.md and $WORD_STUDIES/H7311-ruwm.md for the 'high hand' idiom. Run hebrew_parser.py on Num 15:27-31 to confirm the vocabulary. The distinction is NOT about the nature of the act but the posture of the heart: defiant rebellion vs sincere error. Within the inadvertent category, Lev 4 shows graduated accountability: different sacrifices for priests (4:3-12), rulers (4:22-26), and common persons (4:27-35) — run hebrew_parser.py on Lev 4:2 to parse bishgagah. READ $WORD_STUDIES/H2403-chattaah.md (sin/sin-offering), $WORD_STUDIES/H819-ashmah.md (guilt/guilt-offering), $WORD_STUDIES/H3722-kaphar.md (atonement). The connection to Heb 10:26-29 is critical — hekousios hamartanonton ('willfully sinning') after receiving epignosis of truth parallels Num 15:30's beyad ramah. READ $WORD_STUDIES/G1922-epignosis.md. The high-hand sinner was 'cut off' (karath) — is this the NT equivalent of 'no more sacrifice for sins' (Heb 10:26)?

focus_areas.title: Graduated accountability — Luke 12:47-48, Acts 17:30, 1 Timothy 1:13
focus_areas.what: Establish the NT principle that punishment is proportional to knowledge received and ignorance mitigates but does not eliminate culpability
focus_areas.why: This directly answers whether the deceived mark-recipients are in a different category than those who receive the mark with full knowledge
focus_areas.how: Parse key Greek vocabulary in each passage, trace the knowledge-accountability nexus
focus_areas.narrative: Luke 12:47-48 is the clearest graduated-punishment text — the servant who KNEW (gnous) his master's will and did not do it receives many stripes (daresetai pollas); the one who did not know (me gnous) receives few (oligas). Run greek_parser.py on Luke 12:47-48. The question: does 'few stripes' mean SOME punishment or REDUCED punishment? Acts 17:30 introduces hupereidon (G5237, hapax) — God 'overlooked/winked at' times of ignorance. READ context: this does NOT mean ignorance was sinless, but that God withheld active punishment during the ignorance era. Compare with Rom 3:25 paresis (passing over) — similar concept with different vocabulary. For 1 Tim 1:13, Paul says he obtained mercy (eleethein) BECAUSE he acted 'ignorantly in unbelief' (agnoōn en apistia). Run greek_parser.py on 1 Tim 1:13. The commentators distinguish Paul's ignorance (sincere but wrong — vincible ignorance) from the Pharisees' willful blindness. Cross-reference with John 9:41 ('If ye were blind, ye should have no sin') and John 15:22 ('If I had not come and spoken, they had not had sin'). Also check Jas 4:17 — sin defined relationally to knowledge.

focus_areas.title: Can the mark be resisted? — Revelation 15:2 and the nikao pattern
focus_areas.what: Establish from Revelation's own text that the mark IS resistible and people DO successfully resist it
focus_areas.why: If the mark is irresistible, the third angel's warning is unjust; if resistible, accountability is affirmed
focus_areas.how: Parse Rev 15:2, trace nikao across Revelation, examine the means of overcoming
focus_areas.narrative: Rev 15:2 describes tous nikōntas ek tou thēriou kai ek tēs eikonos autou kai ek tou arithmou tou onomatos autou — 'those overcoming out of the beast and out of his image and out of the number of his name.' READ $WORD_STUDIES/G3528-nikao.md. The present active participle nikōntas indicates ongoing conquering. Note the triple ek (out of) — they overcome the beast, the image, AND the mark (number). Run greek_parser.py on Rev 15:2. Cross-reference with Rev 12:11 — the MEANS of overcoming: 'by the blood of the Lamb and by the word of their testimony.' The prior study (3am-14) noted that Rev 13:15-17 uses hina + subjunctive for the coercion — poiei pantas hina = 'he MAKES all that they should' — purpose clause, not result clause. The mark is ATTEMPTED on all, not successfully imposed on all. Also check Rev 13:7-10: the beast is given authority to 'make war with the saints and to overcome (nikēsai) them' — but this is military/political defeat, not spiritual capitulation. Rev 20:4 confirms the binary: those who reign are defined by 'did not worship...did not receive' — three aorist negations. READ $WORD_STUDIES/G5480-charagma.md and $WORD_STUDIES/G5281-hupomone.md (endurance of the saints).

focus_areas.title: Fair warning guaranteed — the universal gospel and God's provision for seekers
focus_areas.what: Establish from Scripture that God guarantees adequate warning before the mark crisis and makes truth available to all sincere seekers
focus_areas.why: This addresses the justice question: no one can claim they were condemned without opportunity to know truth
focus_areas.how: Collect the universal-gospel and seeker-guarantee passages, trace the light-accountability nexus
focus_areas.narrative: Rev 14:6-7 is the first angel's message — the everlasting gospel proclaimed to 'every nation, kindred, tongue, and people' BEFORE the third angel's warning in 14:9-11. Run greek_parser.py on Rev 14:6 to confirm the universal scope (pan ethnos kai phylen kai glōssan kai laon). Matt 24:14 adds the temporal marker: 'this gospel shall be preached in all the world...and THEN shall the end come.' READ $GRAMMAR_REF/passages/rev-14-6-12.md for the full grammar analysis of the three angels. Rev 18:4 shows God calling His own OUT of Babylon before judgment: 'Come out of her, MY people.' This implies (1) God has people inside Babylon, and (2) He provides the call before destruction. For the seeker-guarantee, collect: Jer 29:13 ('seek me and find me when searching with whole heart'), Matt 7:7-8 ('ask and it shall be given'), John 7:17 ('if any man will do his will, he shall know of the doctrine'), John 10:16 ('other sheep...them also I MUST bring'). Universal light channels: John 1:9 (true light lights every man), Rom 1:19-20 (creation witness), Rom 2:14-15 (conscience), Titus 2:11 (grace appeared to all), John 16:8 (Spirit convicts the world). This establishes that NO ONE reaches the mark crisis without having been reached by truth at some level.

focus_areas.title: Differentiated judgment of the deceived — Revelation 19:20-21
focus_areas.what: Determine whether Revelation differentiates the culpability and fate of the deceivers from the deceived
focus_areas.why: Rev 19:20 explicitly says the mark-receivers were 'deceived' — does this create a different accountability category?
focus_areas.how: Parse Rev 19:20-21, compare fates of beast/false prophet vs their followers
focus_areas.narrative: Rev 19:20 states the false prophet eplanēsen ('deceived,' aorist active of planaō) those who received the mark. The beast and false prophet are thrown alive (zōntes) into the lake of fire (v.20). But the rest (hoi loipoi — their followers, the deceived) are 'killed with the sword' (v.21) — a different fate from the lake of fire. Run greek_parser.py on Rev 19:20-21. READ $GRAMMAR_REF/passages/rev-19-19-21.md if available. The key question: does 'the rest were killed' imply a LESSER judgment than the lake of fire? Or do they simply die before the millennium and face the lake of fire in Rev 20:15? Cross-reference with Rev 20:5 ('the rest of the dead lived not again until the thousand years were finished') and 20:15 ('whosoever was not found written in the book of life was cast into the lake of fire'). The web research (TheBibleSays.com) argues deception is 'a method of recruitment, not an excuse.' Compare: were they deceived because they CHOSE to be (2 Thess 2:10 — did not receive the love of the truth) or despite genuine seeking? The 'repented not' refrain in Rev 9:20-21 and 16:9,11 shows habitual, ongoing defiance — not the profile of sincere seekers who were tragically misled.

focus_areas.title: The close of probation — Rev 22:11, Rev 15:8, Dan 12:1 convergence
focus_areas.what: Evaluate whether Scripture itself (not Ellen White) teaches a pre-advent finalization of human destinies
focus_areas.why: This is the most contested sub-topic. Tinker (evangelical critic) argues close of probation is extra-biblical. The study must show whether the BIBLICAL texts independently teach it.
focus_areas.how: Parse all three key texts in original languages, examine whether they independently converge, engage Tinker's objections
focus_areas.narrative: Three texts must be examined independently. (1) Rev 22:11: ho adikōn adikēsatō eti — is this imperative ('let him be unjust still'), permissive ('allow him'), or declarative ('he will be unjust still')? The aorist imperative form (adikēsatō, agiasatō, etc.) needs careful parsing — run greek_parser.py on Rev 22:11-12. Foucher argues 'let' = permissive, honoring free will. Note the immediate context: v.12 'I come quickly; and my reward is with me' — this PRECEDES the coming, implying finalized destinies BEFORE arrival. (2) Rev 15:8: 'oudeis edynato eiselthein eis ton naon' — 'no one was able to enter the temple.' Run greek_parser.py on Rev 15:8. Does this describe the end of Christ's mediatorial intercession? The temple fills with smoke 'from the glory of God and from his power.' Note: this occurs BEFORE the seven plagues (16:1ff), which are poured out 'without mixture' (akratos, 14:10) — i.e., without mercy's dilution (3am-16 finding). (3) Dan 12:1: 'at that time shall Michael stand up' (ya'amod). Run hebrew_parser.py on Dan 12:1. READ $WORD_STUDIES/H5975-amad.md. Does amad mean 'arise to act' (intervention) or 'stand up = cease activity' (end of intercession)? Note: 'everyone found written in the book shall be delivered' — pre-deliverance determination of who is in the book. Engage Tinker's argument: she claims close of probation contradicts completed atonement (John 6:39-40, Eph 2:8-9). The response must be BIBLICAL, not EGW-based.

focus_areas.title: The Spirit striving has limits — the biblical case for exhausted divine patience
focus_areas.what: Establish from Scripture that God's patience and Spirit-striving have biblically attested limits, after which opportunity ceases
focus_areas.why: This supports the close-of-probation concept from a different angle — not arbitrary cutoff but exhausted patience after persistent rejection
focus_areas.how: Collect passages showing divine patience reaching a limit, trace the 'no remedy' pattern
focus_areas.narrative: Gen 6:3 establishes the foundational principle: 'My spirit shall not always strive (yadon) with man.' Prov 1:24-29 traces the pattern: Wisdom calls (v.24), they refuse, Wisdom 'also will laugh at your calamity' (v.26), then 'they shall call upon me, but I will not answer' (v.28). Run hebrew_parser.py on Gen 6:3. Hos 4:17 is devastatingly brief: 'Ephraim is joined to idols: let him alone.' Compare with 2 Chr 36:15-16: God sent messengers 'rising up betimes' (hashkem — repeated sending) 'until there was no remedy' (en marpē — literally 'no healing'). Jer 7:16 and 15:1 show God TELLING the prophet to stop interceding — 'pray not for this people' and 'though Moses and Samuel stood before me, my mind could not be toward this people.' Isa 22:12-14: 'this iniquity shall not be purged from you till ye die.' Heb 6:4-6: 'impossible to renew unto repentance' after full enlightenment rejected. 1 John 5:16: 'there is a sin unto death: I do not say that he shall pray for it.' Heb 12:16-17: Esau sought repentance 'with tears' but 'found no place of repentance.' Num 14:26-45: Israel's probation expired — they attempted reversal and were defeated. All these show a PATTERN, not an isolated proof-text. The convergence constitutes the biblical case for close of probation.

focus_areas.title: The 'repented not' refrain — mark reception as confirmed defiance, not tragic deception
focus_areas.what: Examine whether Revelation portrays mark-recipients as tragically deceived innocents or as people who persist in knowing defiance
focus_areas.why: This addresses the emotional objection: 'But they were deceived!' — Revelation's own characterization matters
focus_areas.how: Trace the 'repented not' refrain across Revelation, examine the present tenses of mark-worship language
focus_areas.narrative: Revelation uses a recurring refrain: ou metenoesan ('they repented not') — Rev 9:20-21 and Rev 16:9,11. Run greek_parser.py on Rev 9:20-21 and 16:9,11. The verb metanoeo (G3340) indicates they had opportunity and capacity to repent but CHOSE not to. READ $WORD_STUDIES/G3340-metanoeo.md. In the third angel's warning (Rev 14:9-11), the mark-recipients are described with present tenses: ei tis proskunei (whoever WORSHIPS — present active indicative) and lambanei (RECEIVES — present active indicative). These are habitual, ongoing actions — not a one-time tragic mistake. READ $GRAMMAR_REF/passages/rev-14-8-11.md for the detailed grammar. The 'repented not' during the plagues (16:9,11) is especially significant: even under direct divine judgment, they blaspheme God's name and refuse to repent. This is the profile of the 'high hand' sinner of Num 15:30, not the inadvertent sinner of Lev 4. The prior study noted that no passage depicts repentance/recovery AFTER mark reception — the mark represents a finalized choice, not a reversible mistake.

required_word_studies.reason: Core to 2 Thess 2:11 energeian planes — delusion/error/wandering
Supernatural working in 2 Thess 2:9,11 — both Satan's and God's
Full knowledge in Heb 10:26 — link between knowledge and culpability
Mark of the beast — the specific brand/stamp
Seal of God — contrast with charagma
Inadvertent sin — the OT ignorance category (Num 15:25-29)
Ignorance — Acts 17:30, mitigating factor in accountability
Michael 'stands up' in Dan 12:1 — cessation or intervention?
'High hand' (beyad ramah) in Num 15:30 — defiant sin
The mark/sign in Ezek 9:4 — sealing precedent

required_parsing.reason: Causal chain: anth' hon, ouk edexanto (aorist middle), dia touto, pempei (present active)
Paradidomi first occurrence — confirm aorist active indicative
Paradidomi third occurrence + adokimon noun wordplay
Hiphil imperatives: hashmen, hakbed, hasha — causative or permissive?
Hebrew verb behind 'gave them over' — compare with paradidomi
bishgagah vs beyad ramah vocabulary confirmation
bishgagah in context of sin-offering legislation
Aorist imperative forms — imperative, permissive, or declarative?
oudeis edynato eiselthein — imperfect of dynamai; grammatical force
ya'amod (amad) — 'stand up' semantics; kol hannimtza katub basseper
nikountas ek — present participle + triple ek construction
eplanesen (aorist) — differentiated fates of deceiver vs deceived
hupereidon (hapax) + nun paraggellei — past tolerance vs present command
agnoōn en apistia — ignorance clause linked to mercy

required_parallels.reason: OT and NT parallels for judicial-delusion pattern
NT quotations and reinterpretations across the Gospels and Acts 28
NT echoes of high-hand sin in Heb 10:26-29

grammar_references.description: Full grammar analysis of 2 Thess 2:1-12 pericope
Grammar analysis of Rev 14:8-11 (third angel's warning)
Grammar analysis of Rev 14:6-12 (three angels' messages)
Grammar analysis of Heb 10:1-25 (willful sin passage)

missing_library_entries.reason: The verb in 2 Thess 2:10 — ouk edexanto; active reception/refusal
Hapax in Acts 17:30 — 'overlooked/winked at'; critical for graduated accountability
To deceive/lead astray — verb form behind the deception in Rev 19:20
Inadvertent sin/error — noun form central to Num 15 framework
Victory/conquest — noun form of nikao (1 Cor 15:54-57, Rev)
Gospel — the content of the universal warning in Rev 14:6
Grammar concept needed for the 'make fat/make heavy/smear shut' debate
Grammar concept for 'let him be unjust still' — imperative or permissive?

external_corpus_leads.claim: The mark is only imputed after light has been 'clearly set before' the person (ARSH Dec 8, 1859)
external_corpus_leads.directive: Verify via Acts 17:30, John 15:22, Luke 12:47-48 that Scripture supports a light-contingent accountability model

external_corpus_leads.claim: Dan 12:1 + Rev 22:11 converge to mark the close of probation (LIFIN 185.2, CET 187.4)
external_corpus_leads.directive: Verify via Greek/Hebrew text whether these passages INDEPENDENTLY teach a pre-advent finalization of destinies

external_corpus_leads.claim: People are 'given up to strong delusion' because they 'love not the truth' (3LtMs, Ms 9, 1880)
external_corpus_leads.directive: Verify this matches the causal chain in 2 Thess 2:10-12 Greek text
Verify via Hos 4:6, 2 Pet 3:5, and the Num 15 framework whether willful ignorance removes the inadvertent-sin protection

external_corpus_leads.claim: 'There is not a second probation for anyone' (7BC 989.13)
external_corpus_leads.directive: Verify via Matt 25:10-13, Luke 13:24-28, Heb 12:16-17 whether Scripture teaches irrevocable closure of opportunity

external_corpus_leads.claim: Rev 15:5-8 teaches cessation of priestly intercession — 'no one was able to enter the temple' (HWIS p.169)
external_corpus_leads.directive: Verify the Greek of oudeis edynato eiselthein eis ton naon — does this describe end of mediation or something else?

external_corpus_leads.claim: Ezek 9:2-3 + Rev 8:5 + Rev 16:17 + Rev 22:11 form a single close-of-probation event chain (RST p.24)
external_corpus_leads.directive: Verify whether these texts independently converge or whether the linkage is imposed

external_corpus_leads.claim: Warning during sixth plague (Rev 16:15) must precede probation's close (RST p.313)
external_corpus_leads.directive: Verify temporal placement of Rev 16:15 relative to the plague sequence and whether this implies open probation

external_corpus_leads.claim: Rev 10:11 ('thou must prophesy again') proves probation is still open during the interlude (RST p.19)
external_corpus_leads.directive: Verify whether gospel proclamation necessarily implies open probation

external_corpus_leads.claim: Paradidomi in Romans 1:24,26,28 carries 'legal and punitive weight' and 'never implies divine indifference'
external_corpus_leads.directive: Verify against the full semantic range of paradidomi in NT usage — READ $WORD_STUDIES/G3860-paradidomi.md

external_corpus_leads.claim: Three hiphil imperatives in Isa 6:10 (hashmen, hakbed, hasha) represent God confirming people in their chosen state, not directly causing hardness
external_corpus_leads.directive: Verify via hebrew_parser.py on Isa 6:10 and compare how the LXX/NT handle the agency shift

external_corpus_leads.claim: Close of probation is extra-biblical, rooted in Ellen White, and contradicts completed atonement at Calvary
external_corpus_leads.directive: Engage by testing whether Rev 22:11, Rev 15:8, and Dan 12:1 INDEPENDENTLY teach pre-advent finalization without relying on EGW

external_corpus_leads.claim: Rev 22:11 'let' = permissive — probation closes through human choice solidification, not arbitrary divine cutoff
external_corpus_leads.directive: Verify the Greek aorist imperative forms in Rev 22:11 — is the permissive reading grammatically supported?

external_corpus_leads.claim: Culpable ignorance resulting from epistemic vices (overconfidence, laziness, dogmatism) does not exculpate
external_corpus_leads.directive: Apply this framework to 2 Thess 2:10 — the truth-rejecters' ignorance traces to prior 'pleasure in unrighteousness' (v.12)

research_instructions: This study builds on the comprehensive prior study mark-beast-deception-warning but must go DEEPER in three areas: (1) Fresh Greek/Hebrew verification of ALL causal chain claims — do not rely on the prior study's conclusions; re-run parsers on every key verse. (2) The close-of-probation biblical case must be built entirely from Scripture, engaging Tinker's evangelical critique that the doctrine is extra-biblical. The three convergence texts (Rev 22:11, Rev 15:8, Dan 12:1) must each be parsed independently in the original languages before any convergence argument is made. (3) The 'repented not' refrain (Rev 9:20-21; 16:9,11) must be analyzed grammatically to show that Revelation itself characterizes mark-recipients as persistent defiers, not tragic victims of deception. Additionally, the EGW principle that 'where these circumstances are or have been wanting, nothing is imputed' (ARSH Dec 8, 1859) must be verified against the biblical graduated-accountability framework — this is a LEAD, not evidence. The analysis should treat the Tinker critique (close of probation is unbiblical) as the strongest counter-argument and demonstrate from TEXT whether the biblical evidence supports or refutes it.
